header.subtitle: Module 1: Setup & Golden Rules
sec.tools.intro: The first step to boosting productivity with AI is to leave the browser chat. Each interface has a different purpose — using the wrong one creates unnecessary friction and ignores the guardrails you defined for your project.
sec.tools.card.ide.title: IDE
sec.tools.card.ide.subtitle: Cursor / VS Code + Copilot
sec.tools.card.ide.li.1: Generated code already follows your standards
sec.tools.card.ide.li.2: No copy/paste — edits directly in the file
sec.tools.card.ide.li.3: Autocomplete aware of the entire codebase
sec.tools.card.ide.usewhen: Use when: day-to-day development, incremental features, quick edits.
sec.tools.card.cli.title: Agent CLI
sec.tools.card.cli.subtitle: Claude Code / Gemini CLI
sec.tools.card.cli.li.2: Runs commands, tests, and linters on its own
sec.tools.card.cli.li.3: Ideal for large refactors and migrations
sec.tools.card.cli.usewhen: Use when: heavy automation, monorepo refactoring, multi-file tasks.
sec.tools.card.web.title: Web Chat
sec.tools.card.web.subtitle: Claude.ai / ChatGPT
sec.tools.card.web.usewhen: Use when: conceptual exploration, non-code questions, initial architecture brainstorm.
sec.tools.rule3.title: The Rule of 3 Environments
sec.tools.rule3.desc: Experienced devs use all three. Web for thinking, IDE for building, CLI for automating. The most common mistake is trying to write production code in Web — no context, no guardrails, no traceability.
sec.context.intro: Why does AI "start talking nonsense" in long conversations? Because it has a finite short-term memory, measured in tokens. When that space runs out, it discards the oldest information — including your guardrails.
sec.context.token.title: What is a Token?
sec.context.token.desc: Chunks of words processed by the model. On average, 1,000 tokens ≈ 750 words. Claude has a 200k token window — but selective attention matters more than raw size.
sec.context.memory.title: The "Short-Term Memory"
sec.context.memory.desc: When the project + conversation history exceeds the window, the AI starts discarding instructions from the beginning — exactly where your guardrails and project rules live.
sec.context.warning: The Distraction Risk: Sending node_modules, dist, or huge logs consumes valuable tokens and "distracts" the AI's attention from what actually matters.
sec.context.solution: The Solution: Create a .claudeignore (Claude Code) or .cursorignore (Cursor) file to automatically exclude noisy folders from context.
sec.context.caption.dirty: 65% of tokens wasted on noise — the AI can barely "see" the code that matters.
sec.context.caption.clean: With .claudeignore configured: 90% of tokens focused on what matters.
sec.guardrails.intro: Each tool reads project rules from a different file — but the goal is always the same: give the AI a compass so that generated code already comes aligned with your team's standards.
sec.guardrails.claude.note: Automatically read by Claude Code at every session. Place it at the project root and version it with the team.
sec.guardrails.cursor.note: Cursor also supports per-folder rules in .cursor/rules/*.mdc — useful for domain-specific rules.
sec.guardrails.copilot.note: Works with Copilot Chat and autocompletion. Must be at .github/copilot-instructions.md in the repository root.
sec.guardrails.principle: The principle is the same for all: define who the AI is in your project and how it should behave. The tool changes, the concept doesn't. Always version these files alongside your code.
sec.trio.intro: Before asking the AI to write the first line of code, make sure it knows the terrain. Three simple files serve as a context anchor — preventing off-pattern suggestions, wrong library versions, and structures incompatible with the project.
sec.trio.tech.desc: Exact list of libraries, frameworks, and versions used.
sec.trio.tech.goal: Goal: Prevents the AI from suggesting React 17 when you use 19, or installing axios when the project already uses ky.
sec.trio.arch.desc: Explains the structural organization and communication patterns.
sec.trio.arch.goal: Goal: If it says "Feature Folders", the AI won't create loose files in giant /controllers or /views directories.
sec.trio.req.desc: Describes the core business rule, use cases, and edge cases.
sec.trio.req.goal: Goal: Keeps the AI focused on "what" we're building. Prevents it from ignoring critical edge cases or proposing solutions that don't serve the real user.
sec.antipatterns.intro: Knowing what not to do accelerates more than any productivity tip. These are the 4 behaviors that drain AI's potential the most in daily work.
sec.antipatterns.1.wrong.title: The Kitchen-Sink Prompt
sec.antipatterns.1.wrong.desc: Dumping all requirements at once expecting the AI to deliver everything perfectly.
sec.antipatterns.1.wrong.example: "Build a complete authentication system with JWT, refresh token, rate limit, audit logs, 2FA, and Google OAuth integration."
sec.antipatterns.1.right.title: The Surgical Prompt
sec.antipatterns.1.right.desc: One task at a time, validating and building incrementally on what was approved.
sec.antipatterns.1.right.example: "Based on REQUIREMENTS.md, implement the POST /auth/login endpoint. Just email and password login for now."
sec.antipatterns.2.wrong.title: Fix Only in Chat
sec.antipatterns.2.wrong.desc: When the AI uses axios and you want ky, you tell it in chat — and in the next session it uses axios again.
sec.antipatterns.2.wrong.example: "No, use ky instead of axios" → Correct only for this message.
sec.antipatterns.2.right.title: Fix at the Root
sec.antipatterns.2.right.desc: Go to CLAUDE.md and add the rule. The behavior changes permanently for the entire team.
sec.antipatterns.2.right.example:
# In CLAUDE.md:
- HTTP: always use `ky`, never `axios` or `fetch` directly.→ Correct forever.
sec.antipatterns.3.wrong.title: Dragging the Conversation
sec.antipatterns.3.wrong.desc: Continuing a long session even after noticing the AI is forgetting context, generating inconsistencies, or repeating errors.
sec.antipatterns.3.wrong.example: Conversation with 80 messages, AI ignores early rules, suggests patterns that were already corrected before.
sec.antipatterns.3.right.title: Start a New Session
sec.antipatterns.3.right.desc: Detect the drift signal and end it. Open a new session with a "handoff" prompt summarizing what was done.
sec.antipatterns.3.right.example: "Context: we implemented login and refresh token. Tests pass. Next task: implement rate limiting per IP."
sec.antipatterns.4.wrong.title: Accept Without Reading
sec.antipatterns.4.wrong.desc: Approving generated code without understanding the intent — creating hidden tech debt nobody can maintain later.
sec.antipatterns.4.wrong.example: "Looks right, accept all." → 3 weeks later nobody knows why that code exists.
sec.antipatterns.4.right.title: Intent Review
sec.antipatterns.4.right.desc: You don't need to check every comma (the hook does that). But you need to understand why that solution was chosen.
sec.antipatterns.4.right.example: "Explain why you used a Map here instead of an array with find()."
sec.workflow.intro: With tools and base files ready, this is the ritual to start any task. Following this flow transforms the dynamic from "code dictator" to orchestrator.
sec.workflow.step.1.title: IDE Indexing
sec.workflow.step.1.desc: When opening the project, wait for (or force) the IDE to index the repository. The AI can only query what it knows exists. Without indexing, it operates in the dark.
sec.workflow.step.2.title: The "Check-in" — First Prompt
sec.workflow.step.2.desc: Always contextualize the AI with the anchor files before asking for the task. Don't assume it remembers the previous session.
sec.workflow.step.2.example: "Read the files TECH_STACK.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, and CLAUDE.md at the root. Confirm that you understood the patterns before we proceed."
sec.workflow.step.3.title: Structural Validation
sec.workflow.step.3.desc: Evaluate the response. If it suggests something that violates your pattern (e.g., used axios when you use ky), don't just correct it in chat.
sec.workflow.step.3.tip: 💡 The Secret: Go to CLAUDE.md, add the restrictive rule there, and ask the AI to re-read it. You fix the "root" of the behavior, not just a temporary line.
sec.workflow.step.4.title: The Iteration Loop
sec.workflow.step.4.desc: After the first approved response, build incrementally. Each subsequent prompt should reference what was already done.
sec.workflow.step.4.example: "The login endpoint is working and tests pass. Now add the refresh token mechanism as per REQUIREMENTS.md."
sec.workflow.step.5.title: Closing the Session
sec.workflow.step.5.desc: Long sessions degrade. Detect the signs — AI repeating already-corrected errors, ignoring rules, generating inconsistent code — and end it. Open a new session with a handoff prompt:
sec.workflow.step.5.example: "Resuming context: we implemented login + refresh token. Tests pass. Next task: rate limit per IP (see REQUIREMENTS.md, Edge Cases section)."
sec.nextsteps.intro: You don't need to set everything up at once. Four concrete actions you can take today to go from zero setup to a functional environment.
sec.nextsteps.step.1.title: Choose and install your main tool
sec.nextsteps.step.1.desc: For day-to-day development, choose an IDE. For heavy automation, install Claude Code.
sec.nextsteps.step.2.title: Create the guardrails file with at least 3 rules
sec.nextsteps.step.2.desc: Don't wait for the file to be perfect. Start with the minimum and evolve as you find behaviors that need correcting.
sec.nextsteps.step.3.title: Create TECH_STACK.md and ARCHITECTURE.md (minimum viable)
sec.nextsteps.step.3.desc: 10 lines each is enough to start. The goal is that the AI doesn't "guess" your stack.
sec.nextsteps.step.4.title: Run the "Check-in" on your next real task
sec.nextsteps.step.4.desc: Use the template from Step 2 of the Workflow on the next feature you implement. Observe the quality difference in the first response.
sec.nextsteps.step.4.example: Template: "Read TECH_STACK.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, and CLAUDE.md. Confirm that you understood the patterns. Then, [describe the task]."
sec.nextsteps.refs.title: References
